title: Product of array except self
description: Return array where each element is product of all other elements

examples.explanation:
n = 4
For i=0: product=1, j=0 (skip), j=1: product=1*2=2, j=2: product=2*3=6, j=3: product=6*4=24, result[0]=24
For i=1: product=1, j=0: product=1*1=1, j=1 (skip), j=2: product=1*3=3, j=3: product=3*4=12, result[1]=12
For i=2: product=1, j=0: product=1*1=1, j=1: product=1*2=2, j=2 (skip), j=3: product=2*4=8, result[2]=8
For i=3: product=1, j=0: product=1*1=1, j=1: product=1*2=2, j=2: product=2*3=6, j=3 (skip), result[3]=6
Final result: [24, 12, 8, 6]

n = 5
For i=0: product=1, j=0 (skip), j=1: product=1*1=1, j=2: product=1*0=0, j=3: product=0*(-3)=0, j=4: product=0*3=0, result[0]=0
For i=1: product=1, j=0: product=1*(-1)=-1, j=1 (skip), j=2: product=-1*0=0, j=3: product=0*(-3)=0, j=4: product=0*3=0, result[1]=0
For i=2: product=1, j=0: product=1*(-1)=-1, j=1: product=-1*1=-1, j=2 (skip), j=3: product=-1*(-3)=3, j=4: product=3*3=9, result[2]=9
For i=3: product=1, j=0: product=1*(-1)=-1, j=1: product=-1*1=-1, j=2: product=-1*0=0, j=3 (skip), j=4: product=0*3=0, result[3]=0
For i=4: product=1, j=0: product=1*(-1)=-1, j=1: product=-1*1=-1, j=2: product=-1*0=0, j=3: product=0*(-3)=0, j=4 (skip), result[4]=0
Final result: [0, 0, 9, 0, 0]

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: For each element, compute product by iterating through all other elements. Use nested loops to multiply all elements except the current one.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force O(n²) Solution
ladders.desc: Complete implementation using nested loops to compute product of all elements except self for each position.

examples.trace:
n = 4
For i=0: product=1, j=0 (skip), j=1: product=1*2=2, j=2: product=2*3=6, j=3: product=6*4=24, result[0]=24
For i=1: product=1, j=0: product=1*1=1, j=1 (skip), j=2: product=1*3=3, j=3: product=3*4=12, result[1]=12
For i=2: product=1, j=0: product=1*1=1, j=1: product=1*2=2, j=2 (skip), j=3: product=2*4=8, result[2]=8
For i=3: product=1, j=0: product=1*1=1, j=1: product=1*2=2, j=2: product=2*3=6, j=3 (skip), result[3]=6
Final result: [24, 12, 8, 6]

n = 5
For i=0: product=1, j=0 (skip), j=1: product=1*1=1, j=2: product=1*0=0, j=3: product=0*(-3)=0, j=4: product=0*3=0, result[0]=0
For i=1: product=1, j=0: product=1*(-1)=-1, j=1 (skip), j=2: product=-1*0=0, j=3: product=0*(-3)=0, j=4: product=0*3=0, result[1]=0
For i=2: product=1, j=0: product=1*(-1)=-1, j=1: product=-1*1=-1, j=2 (skip), j=3: product=-1*(-3)=3, j=4: product=3*3=9, result[2]=9
For i=3: product=1, j=0: product=1*(-1)=-1, j=1: product=-1*1=-1, j=2: product=-1*0=0, j=3 (skip), j=4: product=0*3=0, result[3]=0
For i=4: product=1, j=0: product=1*(-1)=-1, j=1: product=-1*1=-1, j=2: product=-1*0=0, j=3: product=0*(-3)=0, j=4 (skip), result[4]=0
Final result: [0, 0, 9, 0, 0]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Computing Product for One Index
ladders.desc: Calculate product of all array elements except at a specific index i.

examples.trace:
product = 1
j=0: j≠1 true, product=1*1=1
j=1: j≠1 false, skip
j=2: j≠1 true, product=1*3=3
j=3: j≠1 true, product=3*4=12
Return 12

product = 1
j=0: j≠2 true, product=1*(-1)=-1
j=1: j≠2 true, product=-1*1=-1
j=2: j≠2 false, skip
j=3: j≠2 true, product=-1*(-3)=3
j=4: j≠2 true, product=3*3=9
Return 9

ladders.title: Core Logic — Conditional Product Accumulation
ladders.desc: Multiply array elements while conditionally skipping specific indices.

examples.trace:
product = 1
index=0: condition(0≠1) true, product=1*2=2
index=1: condition(1≠1) false, skip
index=2: condition(2≠1) true, product=2*4=8
Final product = 8

product = 1
index=0: condition(0%2==0) true, product=1*1=1
index=1: condition(1%2==0) false, skip
index=2: condition(2%2==0) true, product=1*2=2
index=3: condition(3%2==0) false, skip
Final product = 2

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal with Index Tracking
ladders.desc: Iterate through an array while keeping track of current index position.
ladders.explanation:
This level addresses the fundamental skill of looping through an array while knowing which element you're currently processing. The intuition is that arrays are indexed collections, and we often need to perform operations based on position. We use a loop variable (typically i, j, or index) that starts at 0 and increments by 1 each iteration until it reaches n-1 (where n is array length). This gives us access to both the current element (array[index]) and its position (index). The algorithm is simple: initialize index to 0, while index < length, process array[index], then increment index. This is crucial because in our problem we need to know when we're at the element to exclude. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing n elements. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L2 (core logic) by providing the looping structure that L2's conditional multiplication operates within. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (using <= instead of < for 0-based indexing), modifying the loop variable incorrectly inside the loop, or not handling empty arrays. The real code pattern is a for loop or while loop with index incrementing.

Pseudocode:
for index from 0 to length(array)-1:
    # Access array[index] and use index as needed
    # Or using while:
index = 0
while index < length(array):
    # Process array[index]
    index = index + 1

examples.trace:
length=3
index=0: array[0]=10
index=1: array[1]=20
index=2: array[2]=30
index=3: stop (3 not < 3)

length=4
index=0: array[0]='a'
index=1: array[1]='b'
index=2: array[2]='c'
index=3: array[3]='d'
index=4: stop (4 not < 4)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Multiplication
ladders.desc: Multiply two integers together to get their product.
ladders.explanation:
This level covers the fundamental arithmetic operation of multiplication. The intuition is that multiplication combines quantities: a × b means adding a to itself b times (or vice versa). For integers, we simply compute the product. The algorithm is the multiplication operation itself. In programming, we use the * operator: result = a * b. This operation is commutative (a×b = b×a), associative ((a×b)×c = a×(b×c)), and has identity element 1 (a×1 = a). Time complexity is O(1) for fixed-size integers. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L3 (array traversal) because as we traverse arrays, we'll multiply elements together. In our problem, we multiply array elements to compute products. Common mistakes include integer overflow (product too large for data type), confusing multiplication with addition, or not understanding that multiplying by 1 leaves a number unchanged (important for initialization). The real code pattern is using the * operator between numeric variables.

Pseudocode:
product = a * b

examples.trace:
-2 × 5 = -10
Negative times positive gives negative
We can think as (-2) + (-2) + (-2) + (-2) + (-2) = -10

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understanding arrays as indexed collections of elements.
ladders.explanation:
This foundational level introduces arrays, which are fundamental data structures in programming. An array is an ordered collection of elements stored contiguously in memory. Each element has an index (position) starting from 0. The intuition is like a row of boxes numbered 0, 1, 2, etc., where each box holds a value. We can access any element using its index: array[0] gives the first element, array[1] the second, etc. Arrays have a fixed length (number of elements). Key concepts: zero-based indexing (first element at index 0), random access (any element accessible in O(1) time), and homogeneity (all elements same type typically). This is crucial for our problem because we need to store and access multiple numbers. Time complexity for access is O(1). Space complexity is O(n) for n elements. This feeds into L4 (multiplication) because we'll multiply values stored in arrays. Common mistakes include accessing indices beyond array bounds (e.g., array[5] for length 5 array - valid indices are 0-4), confusing index with value, or not understanding zero-based indexing. The real code pattern is declaring arrays and accessing elements with square brackets.

Pseudocode:
# Create array
array = [value1, value2, value3]
# Access element at index i
element = array[i]
# Get length
length = len(array)

examples.trace:
Array has 3 elements at indices 0,1,2
Index 0 contains 10
Index 1 contains 20
Index 2 contains 30
Length is 3

Array has 4 elements
Index 0: 'a'
Index 1: 'b'
Index 2: 'c'
Index 3: 'd'
Length is 4
array[2] accesses element at index 2, which is 'c'

approaches.name: Prefix and Suffix Products O(n)
approaches.summary: Compute prefix products (product of all elements to the left) and suffix products (product of all elements to the right) in separate arrays. Then combine them to get the result for each index.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Product of Array Except Self Using Prefix and Suffix Products
ladders.desc: Complete solution computing product of all elements except current using prefix and suffix arrays.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires computing for each element in an array the product of all other elements. A naive approach would multiply all elements for each index, resulting in O(n²) time. The prefix-suffix approach optimizes this to O(n) time and O(n) space by precomputing products. The intuition is to separate the product into two parts: all elements to the left (prefix) and all to the right (suffix). For any index i, the desired product is prefix[i] * suffix[i]. This works because multiplication is associative and commutative, allowing us to break the total product into independent left and right components. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize prefix array of same length as input, set prefix[0] = 1 (no elements to left of first). 2) Iterate from left to right, computing prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] * nums[i-1]. This stores product of all elements before i. 3) Initialize suffix array, set suffix[n-1] = 1 (no elements to right of last). 4) Iterate from right to left, computing suffix[i] = suffix[i+1] * nums[i+1]. 5) Create result array where result[i] = prefix[i] * suffix[i]. Time complexity is O(n) for three passes, space is O(n) for two auxiliary arrays. Connection to L1: This uses the prefix and suffix computation subroutines directly. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle edge cases (empty array, single element), incorrect initialization of prefix/suffix (should be 1, not 0), or off-by-one errors in indices. Real code pattern: def productExceptSelf(nums): n = len(nums); prefix = [1]*n; suffix = [1]*n; for i in range(1,n): prefix[i] = prefix[i-1]*nums[i-1]; for i in range(n-2,-1,-1): suffix[i] = suffix[i+1]*nums[i+1]; return [prefix[i]*suffix[i] for i in range(n)].

examples.trace: Input: nums = [1,2,3,4], n=4. Step 1: Compute prefix array. Initialize prefix[0]=1. i=1: prefix[1]=prefix[0]*nums[0]=1*1=1. i=2: prefix[2]=prefix[1]*nums[1]=1*2=2. i=3: prefix[3]=prefix[2]*nums[2]=2*3=6. Prefix = [1,1,2,6]. Step 2: Compute suffix array. Initialize suffix[3]=1. i=2: suffix[2]=suffix[3]*nums[3]=1*4=4. i=1: suffix[1]=suffix[2]*nums[2]=4*3=12. i=0: suffix[0]=suffix[1]*nums[1]=12*2=24. Suffix = [24,12,4,1]. Step 3: Compute result. result[0]=prefix[0]*suffix[0]=1*24=24. result[1]=1*12=12. result[2]=2*4=8. result[3]=6*1=6. Output: [24,12,8,6].

Input: nums = [-1,1,0,-3,3], n=5. Step 1: Compute prefix. prefix[0]=1. i=1: prefix[1]=prefix[0]*nums[0]=1*(-1)=-1. i=2: prefix[2]=prefix[1]*nums[1]=(-1)*1=-1. i=3: prefix[3]=prefix[2]*nums[2]=(-1)*0=0. i=4: prefix[4]=prefix[3]*nums[3]=0*(-3)=0. Prefix = [1,-1,-1,0,0]. Step 2: Compute suffix. suffix[4]=1. i=3: suffix[3]=suffix[4]*nums[4]=1*3=3. i=2: suffix[2]=suffix[3]*nums[3]=3*(-3)=-9. i=1: suffix[1]=suffix[2]*nums[2]=(-9)*0=0. i=0: suffix[0]=suffix[1]*nums[1]=0*1=0. Suffix = [0,0,-9,3,1]. Step 3: Compute result. result[0]=prefix[0]*suffix[0]=1*0=0. result[1]=(-1)*0=0. result[2]=(-1)*(-9)=9. result[3]=0*3=0. result[4]=0*1=0. Output: [0,0,9,0,0].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Computing Prefix and Suffix Product Arrays
ladders.desc: Generate prefix (left products) and suffix (right products) arrays from an input array.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core computational step in the full solution. The intuition is to precompute cumulative products from both directions to avoid redundant multiplications. For prefix products: we want prefix[i] to be the product of all elements from index 0 to i-1 (i.e., all elements to the left of i). This can be built iteratively: start with prefix[0]=1 (since there are no elements to the left of index 0), then for each i from 1 to n-1, set prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] * nums[i-1]. This works because prefix[i-1] already contains product of elements 0 to i-2, and multiplying by nums[i-1] adds the next left element. Similarly, for suffix products: suffix[i] should be product of elements from i+1 to n-1 (all to the right). Initialize suffix[n-1]=1 (no elements to right of last index), then iterate from n-2 down to 0, setting suffix[i] = suffix[i+1] * nums[i+1]. Time complexity is O(n) for each array, space is O(n) for storage. Connection to L0: These arrays are directly multiplied in L0 to get the final result. Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of breaking the product into left and right parts. Common mistakes include incorrect iteration direction (prefix must go left-to-right, suffix right-to-left), wrong initial values (should be 1, not 0 or nums[0]), or index errors (using nums[i] instead of nums[i-1] for prefix). Real code pattern: def computePrefixSuffix(nums): n = len(nums); prefix = [1]*n; suffix = [1]*n; for i in range(1,n): prefix[i] = prefix[i-1]*nums[i-1]; for i in range(n-2,-1,-1): suffix[i] = suffix[i+1]*nums[i+1]; return prefix, suffix.

examples.trace: Input: nums=[1,2,3,4], n=4. Compute prefix: Initialize prefix=[1,1,1,1]. i=1: prefix[1]=prefix[0]*nums[0]=1*1=1. i=2: prefix[2]=prefix[1]*nums[1]=1*2=2. i=3: prefix[3]=prefix[2]*nums[2]=2*3=6. Prefix=[1,1,2,6]. Compute suffix: Initialize suffix=[1,1,1,1]. i=2: suffix[2]=suffix[3]*nums[3]=1*4=4. i=1: suffix[1]=suffix[2]*nums[2]=4*3=12. i=0: suffix[0]=suffix[1]*nums[1]=12*2=24. Suffix=[24,12,4,1]. Output: prefix=[1,1,2,6], suffix=[24,12,4,1].

Input: nums=[2,3,5], n=3. Compute prefix: Initialize prefix=[1,1,1]. i=1: prefix[1]=prefix[0]*nums[0]=1*2=2. i=2: prefix[2]=prefix[1]*nums[1]=2*3=6. Prefix=[1,2,6]. Compute suffix: Initialize suffix=[1,1,1]. i=1: suffix[1]=suffix[2]*nums[2]=1*5=5. i=0: suffix[0]=suffix[1]*nums[1]=5*3=15. Suffix=[15,5,1]. Output: prefix=[1,2,6], suffix=[15,5,1].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Breaking Product into Left and Right Components
ladders.desc: Understanding that product except self equals product of left elements times product of right elements.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight that enables the O(n) solution. For any element at index i in an array, the product of all other elements can be decomposed into two independent parts: the product of all elements to the left (indices 0 to i-1) and the product of all elements to the right (indices i+1 to n-1). Mathematically, if the array is nums of length n, then result[i] = (product of nums[0...i-1]) * (product of nums[i+1...n-1]). This works because multiplication is associative (grouping doesn't matter) and commutative (order doesn't matter), so the total product of all elements except nums[i] can be split at index i. The intuition is to avoid recomputing the entire product for each i by precomputing these left and right products. This reduces the problem from O(n²) to O(n). Step-by-step: 1) For each i, compute left_product[i] as cumulative product from start to i-1. 2) Compute right_product[i] as cumulative product from i+1 to end. 3) Combine as result[i] = left_product[i] * right_product[i]. Time complexity for computing left and right products is O(n) each, space is O(n) to store them. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented by computing prefix and suffix arrays. Connection to L3: This builds on the concept of cumulative products. Common mistakes include misunderstanding the boundaries (left product for i=0 should be 1, right for i=n-1 should be 1), or trying to compute total product and divide by nums[i] (fails with zeros). Real code pattern: Concept only, no full code. Example: For nums=[a,b,c,d], result[1] = left_product[1] (which is a) * right_product[1] (which is c*d).

examples.trace: Given nums=[2,3,4], n=3, focus on index i=1 (value 3). Step 1: Compute left product for i=1. Elements to left: indices 0 to 0, which is [2]. Product = 2. Step 2: Compute right product for i=1. Elements to right: indices 2 to 2, which is [4]. Product = 4. Step 3: Combine: result[1] = left product * right product = 2 * 4 = 8. This matches product except self: product of all elements except nums[1] = 2 * 4 = 8.

Given nums=[1,2,3,4,5], n=5, focus on index i=2 (value 3). Step 1: Left product for i=2. Elements to left: indices 0 to 1, values [1,2]. Product = 1*2 = 2. Step 2: Right product for i=2. Elements to right: indices 3 to 4, values [4,5]. Product = 4*5 = 20. Step 3: Combine: result[2] = 2 * 20 = 40. Verify: product except self = 1*2*4*5 = 40.

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Cumulative Product Array
ladders.desc: Generate an array where each element is product of all previous elements up to that point.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for computing prefix and suffix arrays. A cumulative product array (also called running product) stores at each index the product of all elements from the start up to that index. For an array nums of length n, define cumul[i] = product of nums[0] * nums[1] * ... * nums[i]. This can be computed iteratively: initialize cumul[0] = nums[0], then for i from 1 to n-1, set cumul[i] = cumul[i-1] * nums[i]. The intuition is to reuse previous computations to avoid redundant multiplications, similar to cumulative sum but with multiplication. This reduces computing product for any range from O(n) to O(1) if you have cumulative products, though here we adapt it for prefix/suffix. For prefix products in the main problem, we need product of elements before i, which is cumul[i-1] if cumul includes up to i-1. Time complexity is O(n), space is O(n). Connection to L2: Cumulative products are used to derive left and right products efficiently. Connection to L4: This builds on basic multiplication operations. Common mistakes include not handling empty arrays, initializing cumul[0] incorrectly (should be nums[0], not 1), or confusion between inclusive vs exclusive cumulative products. Real code pattern: def cumulativeProduct(nums): if not nums: return []; n = len(nums); cumul = [0]*n; cumul[0] = nums[0]; for i in range(1,n): cumul[i] = cumul[i-1] * nums[i]; return cumul.

examples.trace: Input: nums=[1,2,3,4], n=4. Initialize cumul array of length 4. Step 1: cumul[0] = nums[0] = 1. Step 2: i=1, cumul[1] = cumul[0] * nums[1] = 1 * 2 = 2. Step 3: i=2, cumul[2] = cumul[1] * nums[2] = 2 * 3 = 6. Step 4: i=3, cumul[3] = cumul[2] * nums[3] = 6 * 4 = 24. Output: [1,2,6,24].

Input: nums=[2,3,5], n=3. Initialize cumul array of length 3. Step 1: cumul[0] = nums[0] = 2. Step 2: i=1, cumul[1] = cumul[0] * nums[1] = 2 * 3 = 6. Step 3: i=2, cumul[2] = cumul[1] * nums[2] = 6 * 5 = 30. Output: [2,6,30].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterative Array Multiplication
ladders.desc: Multiply elements of an array sequentially using a loop.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves traversing an array and multiplying its elements together. It is the building block for cumulative products and prefix/suffix computations. The intuition is simple: start with an initial product (often 1 for multiplication identity), then for each element in the array, update the product by multiplying it with the current element. This is done using a loop that iterates through indices. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize a variable 'product' to 1 (since 1 is the multiplicative identity). 2) Iterate through each element 'num' in the array. 3) In each iteration, update product = product * num. 4) After the loop, 'product' contains the product of all elements. Time complexity is O(n) for n elements, space is O(1) aside from input. Connection to L3: This operation is repeated in cumulative product computation. Connection to L5: It relies on understanding multiplication and loops. Common mistakes include initializing product to 0 (which would always result in 0), not handling empty arrays (should return 1 or handle separately), or integer overflow in languages without big integers. Real code pattern: def multiplyArray(arr): product = 1; for num in arr: product *= num; return product.

examples.trace: Input array: [1,2,3,4]. Step 1: Initialize product = 1. Step 2: Iterate through elements. First element: 1, product = 1 * 1 = 1. Second element: 2, product = 1 * 2 = 2. Third element: 3, product = 2 * 3 = 6. Fourth element: 4, product = 6 * 4 = 24. Step 3: Return product = 24.
Input array: [2,3,5]. Step 1: Initialize product = 1. Step 2: Iterate. First: 2, product = 1 * 2 = 2. Second: 3, product = 2 * 3 = 6. Third: 5, product = 6 * 5 = 30. Return 30.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Multiplication and Array Traversal
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of multiplication and looping through arrays.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite, designed for beginners. Multiplication is an arithmetic operation where numbers are combined to get a product. For example, 2 * 3 = 6. Key properties: identity element is 1 (any number times 1 is itself), zero property (any number times 0 is 0), and it is associative and commutative. An array is a data structure that stores elements in order, accessible by index starting from 0. Traversing an array means visiting each element sequentially, typically using a loop. In programming, a 'for' loop is common: for i in range(len(array)): access array[i]. The intuition for this ladder is to combine these basics: use a loop to access each array element and multiply them together. Step-by-step: 1) Understand the array: e.g., [2,3,4] has elements 2 at index 0, 3 at index 1, 4 at index 2. 2) Set up a variable to hold the running product, start at 1. 3) Use a loop to go through indices 0,1,2. 4) At each step, multiply running product by the current element. Time complexity is linear in number of elements, space is constant. Connection to L4: This directly enables iterative array multiplication. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loops, misunderstanding array indices, or not initializing the product variable. Real code pattern: Very basic pseudocode: product = 1; for i from 0 to length-1: product = product * array[i]; print product.

examples.trace: Given array [2,3]. Step 1: Understand array: two elements, index 0 is 2, index 1 is 3. Step 2: Initialize product = 1. Step 3: Start loop. First iteration: i=0, access array[0]=2, product = 1 * 2 = 2. Second iteration: i=1, access array[1]=3, product = 2 * 3 = 6. Step 4: End loop, product = 6. Explanation: Multiplication: 2 * 3 = 6. Traversal: visited indices 0 and 1 in order.

Array [1,2,3]. Step 1: Elements: index0=1, index1=2, index2=3. Step 2: product = 1. Step 3: Loop i=0: array[0]=1, product=1*1=1. i=1: array[1]=2, product=1*2=2. i=2: array[2]=3, product=2*3=6. Step 4: Result 6. Multiplication: 1*2*3=6. Traversal: went through all three indices.

approaches.name: Space-Optimized Prefix-Suffix O(n)
approaches.summary: Compute the result array by first storing prefix products directly in it, then iterating from the end to multiply by suffix products using a running variable. This avoids extra arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Space-Optimized Prefix-Suffix Product Array
ladders.desc: Complete solution computing product of all elements except self using O(1) extra space
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the space-optimized prefix-suffix approach for the 'Product of Array Except Self' problem. The intuition is that we can compute the product of all elements except the current one by multiplying the product of all elements to the left (prefix) with the product of all elements to the right (suffix). The key optimization is that we can compute this in-place without allocating additional arrays beyond the result array. We first compute prefix products directly in the result array by iterating from left to right, storing the running product of all previous elements at each position. Then we iterate from right to left, maintaining a running suffix product variable, and multiply each result element by this suffix product. This approach works because at position i, result[i] initially contains the product of all elements from index 0 to i-1 (prefix), and then we multiply it by the product of all elements from i+1 to n-1 (suffix). The time complexity is O(n) since we make two passes through the array. The space complexity is O(1) excluding the output array, as we only use a single variable for the suffix product. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete two-pass algorithm. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize the suffix product correctly, not handling the first element properly in the prefix pass, or incorrectly updating the suffix product before/after multiplication. The algorithm must handle edge cases like arrays with zeros, single elements, or negative numbers correctly.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize result array of same length as input. Step 2: First pass (prefix): result[0] = 1 (no elements before index 0). result[1] = result[0] * nums[0] = 1 * 1 = 1. result[2] = result[1] * nums[1] = 1 * 2 = 2. result[3] = result[2] * nums[2] = 2 * 3 = 6. After prefix pass: result = [1, 1, 2, 6]. Step 3: Second pass (suffix): Initialize suffix = 1. For i = 3: result[3] = result[3] * suffix = 6 * 1 = 6. Update suffix = suffix * nums[3] = 1 * 4 = 4. For i = 2: result[2] = result[2] * suffix = 2 * 4 = 8. Update suffix = suffix * nums[2] = 4 * 3 = 12. For i = 1: result[1] = result[1] * suffix = 1 * 12 = 12. Update suffix = suffix * nums[1] = 12 * 2 = 24. For i = 0: result[0] = result[0] * suffix = 1 * 24 = 24. Update suffix = suffix * nums[0] = 24 * 1 = 24. Final result: [24, 12, 8, 6].

Step 1: Initialize result array of length 5. Step 2: First pass (prefix): result[0] = 1. result[1] = result[0] * nums[0] = 1 * (-1) = -1. result[2] = result[1] * nums[1] = -1 * 1 = -1. result[3] = result[2] * nums[2] = -1 * 0 = 0. result[4] = result[3] * nums[3] = 0 * (-3) = 0. After prefix pass: result = [1, -1, -1, 0, 0]. Step 3: Second pass (suffix): Initialize suffix = 1. For i = 4: result[4] = 0 * 1 = 0. Update suffix = 1 * 3 = 3. For i = 3: result[3] = 0 * 3 = 0. Update suffix = 3 * (-3) = -9. For i = 2: result[2] = -1 * (-9) = 9. Update suffix = -9 * 0 = 0. For i = 1: result[1] = -1 * 0 = 0. Update suffix = 0 * 1 = 0. For i = 0: result[0] = 1 * 0 = 0. Update suffix = 0 * (-1) = 0. Final result: [0, 0, 9, 0, 0].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Two-Pass In-Place Product Computation
ladders.desc: The core two-pass algorithm that computes products without extra arrays
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the two-pass algorithm that forms the heart of the space-optimized solution. The intuition is that we can compute the required products by making two linear passes through the array: one from left to right to compute prefix products, and one from right to left to incorporate suffix products. In the first pass, we build an array where each element contains the product of all elements to its left. In the second pass, we traverse from the end while maintaining a running product of elements to the right (suffix), and multiply each element by this suffix product. This works because the product except self at position i equals (product of elements 0 to i-1) × (product of elements i+1 to n-1). By computing prefixes first and then incorporating suffixes from the right, we avoid needing separate prefix and suffix arrays. The time complexity is O(n) for the two passes. The space complexity is O(1) beyond the output array since we only use a single variable for the suffix product. This connects to L0 as the complete implementation, and to L2 which focuses on the prefix-suffix decomposition concept. Common mistakes include incorrect initialization of the prefix array (first element should be 1), forgetting to update the suffix variable in the correct order, or off-by-one errors in the indices. The algorithm must carefully handle the boundaries: at the start of the prefix pass, there are no elements to the left, so we use 1; similarly at the end of the suffix pass, there are no elements to the right, so we start with suffix = 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize result array of length 3. Step 2: Prefix pass: result[0] = 1. result[1] = result[0] * nums[0] = 1 * 1 = 1. result[2] = result[1] * nums[1] = 1 * 2 = 2. After prefix: result = [1, 1, 2]. Step 3: Suffix pass: suffix = 1. i = 2: result[2] = 2 * 1 = 2. suffix = 1 * 3 = 3. i = 1: result[1] = 1 * 3 = 3. suffix = 3 * 2 = 6. i = 0: result[0] = 1 * 6 = 6. suffix = 6 * 1 = 6. Final: [6, 3, 2].

Step 1: Initialize result array of length 3. Step 2: Prefix pass: result[0] = 1. result[1] = 1 * 2 = 2. result[2] = 2 * 4 = 8. After prefix: result = [1, 2, 8]. Step 3: Suffix pass: suffix = 1. i = 2: result[2] = 8 * 1 = 8. suffix = 1 * 6 = 6. i = 1: result[1] = 2 * 6 = 12. suffix = 6 * 4 = 24. i = 0: result[0] = 1 * 24 = 24. suffix = 24 * 2 = 48. Final: [24, 12, 8].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Prefix-Suffix Decomposition
ladders.desc: The mathematical insight that product except self equals prefix × suffix
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core mathematical insight that makes the algorithm possible: for any element at index i in an array nums of length n, the product of all elements except nums[i] can be expressed as the product of two parts: prefix[i] (product of all elements before i) multiplied by suffix[i] (product of all elements after i). Formally, result[i] = (∏_{j=0}^{i-1} nums[j]) × (∏_{j=i+1}^{n-1} nums[j]). This decomposition is powerful because it breaks a complex O(n²) problem (computing n different products, each excluding one element) into two simpler O(n) problems: computing all prefixes and all suffixes. The intuition is that instead of recomputing the product for each position from scratch, we can reuse computations. If we compute all prefix products in one forward pass and all suffix products in one backward pass, we can combine them to get the final answer. This works because multiplication is associative and commutative, allowing us to group the products this way. The time complexity for computing all prefixes is O(n), and for all suffixes is O(n), giving O(n) total. The space complexity would be O(n) if we store both prefix and suffix arrays, but the optimization in higher levels reduces this to O(1). This connects to L1 which implements this decomposition, and to L3 which deals with computing running products. Common mistakes include misunderstanding the boundaries (prefix for first element is 1, suffix for last element is 1), or incorrectly applying the formula when there are zeros in the array. The decomposition still works with zeros because 0 × anything = 0, but special care is needed in implementation.

examples.trace: For array [2, 3, 4, 5]: Prefix products: prefix[0] = 1 (no elements before). prefix[1] = 2. prefix[2] = 2×3 = 6. prefix[3] = 6×4 = 24. Suffix products: suffix[3] = 1 (no elements after). suffix[2] = 5. suffix[1] = 5×4 = 20. suffix[0] = 20×3 = 60. Now result[i] = prefix[i] × suffix[i]: result[0] = 1 × 60 = 60. result[1] = 2 × 20 = 40. result[2] = 6 × 5 = 30. result[3] = 24 × 1 = 24. This matches the expected output.

For array [1, 0, 2]: Prefix products: prefix[0] = 1. prefix[1] = 1×1 = 1. prefix[2] = 1×0 = 0. Suffix products: suffix[2] = 1. suffix[1] = 2. suffix[0] = 2×0 = 0. Result: result[0] = 1 × 0 = 0. result[1] = 1 × 2 = 2. result[2] = 0 × 1 = 0. This shows how zeros are handled: when the current element is 0 (at index 1), the product except self is just the product of other elements (1×2=2). When other elements contain 0, the result becomes 0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Running Products
ladders.desc: How to efficiently compute cumulative products in an array
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on computing running (cumulative) products in an array, which is essential for both the prefix and suffix computations. The intuition is that instead of computing each product independently, we can compute them sequentially by multiplying the current element with the previous product. For prefix products: we start with product = 1 (the multiplicative identity), then for each position i from 0 to n-1, we set prefix[i] = product, then update product = product × nums[i]. This gives us the product of all elements before position i. For suffix products: we start with product = 1, then for each position i from n-1 down to 0, we set suffix[i] = product, then update product = product × nums[i]. This gives us the product of all elements after position i. The key insight is the recurrence relation: prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] × nums[i-1] for i > 0, with prefix[0] = 1. Similarly, suffix[i] = suffix[i+1] × nums[i+1] for i < n-1, with suffix[n-1] = 1. This allows O(n) computation instead of O(n²) if done naively. The time complexity is O(n) for each pass. The space complexity is O(n) if storing all products, but can be O(1) if computed on the fly. This connects to L2 which uses these running products, and to L4 which deals with basic multiplication operations. Common mistakes include initializing with 0 instead of 1 (since 0 × anything = 0), incorrect update order (should store current product before updating), or off-by-one errors in the recurrence. The algorithm works because multiplication is associative: (a×b)×c = a×(b×c), allowing us to build products incrementally.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize product = 1. Step 2: For i = 0: prefix[0] = product = 1. Update product = 1 × 2 = 2. Step 3: For i = 1: prefix[1] = product = 2. Update product = 2 × 3 = 6. Step 4: For i = 2: prefix[2] = product = 6. Update product = 6 × 4 = 24. The prefix array is [1, 2, 6], representing: before index 0: 1 (empty product), before index 1: 2, before index 2: 2×3=6.

Step 1: Initialize product = 1. Step 2: For i = 2 (from end): suffix[2] = product = 1. Update product = 1 × 4 = 4. Step 3: For i = 1: suffix[1] = product = 4. Update product = 4 × 3 = 12. Step 4: For i = 0: suffix[0] = product = 12. Update product = 12 × 2 = 24. The suffix array is [12, 4, 1], representing: after index 0: 3×4=12, after index 1: 4, after index 2: 1 (empty product).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Traversal and Multiplication
ladders.desc: Fundamental skills of iterating through arrays and multiplying elements
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic operations needed for the algorithm: iterating through arrays and performing multiplication. Array traversal is the process of accessing each element of an array sequentially, either from start to end (forward traversal) or from end to start (backward traversal). In forward traversal, we typically use an index i that goes from 0 to n-1, accessing elements as nums[i]. In backward traversal, i goes from n-1 down to 0. Multiplication is the arithmetic operation of combining numbers, and it has key properties: it's associative ( (a×b)×c = a×(b×c) ), commutative ( a×b = b×a ), and has identity element 1 ( a×1 = a ). For the algorithm, we need to multiply elements together to form products. The basic pattern is to initialize a product variable to 1 (the multiplicative identity), then for each element, multiply the product by that element. For example, to compute the product of all elements in an array: product = 1; for each element x in array: product = product × x. This works because starting with 1 ensures the first multiplication gives the first element, and subsequent multiplications accumulate all elements. The time complexity for traversing an array is O(n), and multiplication is O(1) per operation. The space complexity is O(1) for the product variable. This connects to L3 which uses these operations to compute running products, and to L5 which covers even more basic concepts. Common mistakes include using 0 as initial value (which would make all products 0), incorrect loop boundaries (off-by-one errors), or confusing array indices. It's also important to understand that multiplication can overflow with large numbers, though that's not the focus here.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize product = 1. Step 2: First element 2: product = 1 × 2 = 2. Step 3: Second element 3: product = 2 × 3 = 6. Step 4: Third element 4: product = 6 × 4 = 24. Final product is 24.

Step 1: Initialize product = 1. Step 2: Backward traversal: i = 2, element 5: product = 1 × 5 = 5. Step 3: i = 1, element 0: product = 5 × 0 = 0. Step 4: i = 0, element 1: product = 0 × 1 = 0. Final product is 0, showing that multiplication by zero dominates.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Multiplication Basics
ladders.desc: Understanding what arrays are and how multiplication works
ladders.explanation: This foundational level covers the absolute basics needed to understand the problem: arrays and multiplication. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements, each identified by an index. In most programming languages, arrays are zero-indexed, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. For example, an array nums = [2, 3, 4] has nums[0] = 2, nums[1] = 3, nums[2] = 4. The length of this array is 3. Arrays allow random access to elements using their indices. Multiplication is one of the four basic arithmetic operations. It combines two numbers to produce a product. For example, 2 × 3 = 6. Key properties: 1) The product of a number and 1 is the number itself (identity property). 2) The product of any number and 0 is 0. 3) Multiplication is commutative: a × b = b × a. 4) Multiplication is associative: (a × b) × c = a × (b × c). These properties are crucial for the algorithm because they allow us to group products in different ways without changing the result. For instance, to compute the product of [2, 3, 4], we can do (2×3)×4 or 2×(3×4), both equal 24. In programming, we typically use the * operator for multiplication. Understanding how to access array elements and perform multiplication is the first step toward solving the product problem. This connects to L4 which combines these basics into array traversal and multiplication patterns. Common beginner mistakes include confusing array indices (thinking index 1 is the first element), forgetting that multiplication by 0 gives 0, or not understanding that we need to initialize product variables to 1 (not 0) when accumulating products.

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify array elements: index 0: 5, index 1: 6, index 2: 7. Step 2: Multiply 5 and 6: 5 × 6 = 30. Step 3: Multiply result by 7: 30 × 7 = 210. Alternatively, multiply in different order: 6×7=42, then 42×5=210. Both give same result due to commutativity and associativity.

Step 1: Multiply all elements: 1 × 0 × 3. Step 2: Due to associativity, we can group as (1×0)×3 or 1×(0×3). Step 3: 1×0 = 0, then 0×3 = 0. Or 0×3 = 0, then 1×0 = 0. Step 4: The product is 0 because any multiplication involving 0 gives 0. This shows the dominance of zero in multiplication.
